question:
Problem A-6. Let \( n \) be a positive integer, and let \( f(n) \) denote the last nonzero digit in the decimal expansion of \( n! \). For instance, \( f(5)=2 \).
(a) Show that if \( a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{k} \) are distinct nonnegative integers, then \( f\left(5^{a_{1}}+5^{a_{2}}+\cdots+5^{a_{k}}\right) \) depends only on the sum \( a_{1}+a_{2}+\cdots+a_{k} \).
(b) Assuming part (a), we can define
\[
g(s)=f\left(5^{a_{1}}+5^{a_{2}}+\cdots+5^{a_{k}}\right)
\]
where \( s=a_{1}+a_{2}+\cdots+a_{k} \). Find the least positive integer \( p \) for which
\[
g(s)=g(s+p), \quad \text { for all } s \geqslant 1
\]
or else show that no such \( p \) exists.
solution:
A-6.
(a) All congruences are modulo 10 .

Lemma. \( f(5 n) \equiv 2^{n} f(n) \).
Proof. We have
(*)
\[
(5 n)!=10^{n} n!\prod_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{(5 i+1)(5 i+2)(5 i+3)(5 i+4)}{2} .
\]

If \( i \) is even, then
\[
\frac{1}{2}(5 i+1)(5 i+2)(5 i+3)(5 i+4) \equiv \frac{1}{2}(1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 4) \equiv 2,
\]
and if \( i \) is odd, then
\[
\frac{1}{2}(5 i+1)(5 i+2)(5 i+3)(5 i+4) \equiv \frac{1}{2}(6 \cdot 7 \cdot 8 \cdot 9) \equiv 2
\]

Thus the entire product above is congruent to \( 2^{n} \). From (*) it is clear that the largest power of 10 dividing ( \( 5 n \) )! is the same as the largest power of 10 dividing \( 10^{n} n \) !, and the proof follows.

We now show by induction on \( 5^{a_{1}}+\cdots+5^{a_{k}} \) that
\[
f\left(5^{a_{1}}+\cdots+5^{a_{k}}\right) \equiv 2^{a_{1}+\cdots+a_{k}}
\]
(which depends only on \( a_{1}+\cdots+a_{k} \) as desired).
This is true for \( 5^{a_{1}}+\cdots+5^{a_{k}}=1 \), since \( f\left(5^{0}\right) \equiv 2^{0} \equiv 1 \).
Case 1. All \( a_{i}>0 \). By the lemma and induction,
\[
\begin{aligned}
f\left(5^{a_{1}}+\cdots+5^{a_{k}}\right) & \equiv 2^{5^{a_{1}-1}+\cdots+5^{a_{k}-1}} f\left(5^{a_{1}-1}+\cdots+5^{a_{k}-1}\right) \\
& \equiv 2^{k} \cdot 2^{\left(a_{1}-1\right)+\cdots+\left(a_{k}-1\right)} \quad\left(\text { since } 2^{5^{\prime}} \equiv 2 \text { for } i \geqslant 0\right) \\
& \equiv 2^{a_{1}+\cdots+a_{k}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Case 2. Some \( a_{1}=0 \), say \( a_{1}=0 \). Now
\[
(1+5 m)!=(1+5 m)(5 m)!,
\]
so \( f(1+5 m) \equiv(1+5 m) f(5 m) \). But \( f(5 m) \) is even for \( m \geqslant 1 \) since \( (5 m)! \) is divisible by a higher power of 2 than of 5 . But
\[
(1+5 m) \cdot(2 j) \equiv 2 j,
\]
so \( f(1+5 m) \equiv f(5 m) \). Letting \( m=5^{a_{2}-1}+\cdots+5^{a_{k}-1} \), the proof follows by induction.
(b) The least \( p \geqslant 1 \) for which \( 2^{s+p} \equiv 2^{2} \) for all \( s \geqslant 1 \) is \( p=4 \).

descriptive_long.question:
Problem A-6. Let \( \text{posintn} \) be a positive integer, and let \( \text{lastdigit}(\text{posintn}) \) denote the last nonzero digit in the decimal expansion of \( \text{posintn}! \). For instance, \( \text{lastdigit}(5)=2 \).
(a) Show that if \( \text{firstexp}, \text{secondex}, \ldots, \text{kthexpon} \) are distinct nonnegative integers, then
\[
\text{lastdigit}\left(5^{\text{firstexp}}+5^{\text{secondex}}+\cdots+5^{\text{kthexpon}}\right)
\]
depends only on the sum \( \text{firstexp}+\text{secondex}+\cdots+\text{kthexpon} \).
(b) Assuming part (a), we can define
\[
\text{sumfunct}(\text{sumsindex})=\text{lastdigit}\left(5^{\text{firstexp}}+5^{\text{secondex}}+\cdots+5^{\text{kthexpon}}\right)
\]
where \( \text{sumsindex}=\text{firstexp}+\text{secondex}+\cdots+\text{kthexpon} \). Find the least positive integer \( \text{periodic} \) for which
\[
\text{sumfunct}(\text{sumsindex})=\text{sumfunct}(\text{sumsindex}+\text{periodic}), \quad \text { for all } \text{sumsindex} \geqslant 1
\]
or else show that no such \( \text{periodic} \) exists.
descriptive_long.solution:
A-6.
(a) All congruences are modulo 10.

Lemma. \( \text{lastdigit}(5\,\text{posintn}) \equiv 2^{\text{posintn}} \text{lastdigit}(\text{posintn}) \).

Proof. We have
(*)
\[
(5 \text{posintn})!=10^{\text{posintn}} \; \text{posintn}!\prod_{\text{loopindx}=0}^{\text{posintn}-1} \frac{(5 \text{loopindx}+1)(5 \text{loopindx}+2)(5 \text{loopindx}+3)(5 \text{loopindx}+4)}{2} .
\]

If \( \text{loopindx} \) is even, then
\[
\frac{1}{2}(5 \text{loopindx}+1)(5 \text{loopindx}+2)(5 \text{loopindx}+3)(5 \text{loopindx}+4) \equiv \frac{1}{2}(1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 4) \equiv 2,
\]
and if \( \text{loopindx} \) is odd, then
\[
\frac{1}{2}(5 \text{loopindx}+1)(5 \text{loopindx}+2)(5 \text{loopindx}+3)(5 \text{loopindx}+4) \equiv \frac{1}{2}(6 \cdot 7 \cdot 8 \cdot 9) \equiv 2.
\]

Thus the entire product above is congruent to \( 2^{\text{posintn}} \). From (*) it is clear that the largest power of 10 dividing \( (5 \text{posintn})! \) is the same as the largest power of 10 dividing \( 10^{\text{posintn}}\, \text{posintn}! \), and the proof follows.

We now show by induction on \( 5^{\text{firstexp}}+\cdots+5^{\text{kthexpon}} \) that
\[
\text{lastdigit}\left(5^{\text{firstexp}}+\cdots+5^{\text{kthexpon}}\right) \equiv 2^{\text{firstexp}+\cdots+\text{kthexpon}}
\]
(which depends only on \( \text{firstexp}+\cdots+\text{kthexpon} \) as desired).
This is true for \( 5^{\text{firstexp}}+\cdots+5^{\text{kthexpon}}=1 \), since \( \text{lastdigit}\left(5^{0}\right) \equiv 2^{0} \equiv 1 \).

Case 1. All \( \text{firstexp},\ldots,\text{kthexpon} >0 \). By the lemma and induction,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{lastdigit}\left(5^{\text{firstexp}}+\cdots+5^{\text{kthexpon}}\right) & \equiv 2^{5^{\text{firstexp}-1}+\cdots+5^{\text{kthexpon}-1}} \text{lastdigit}\left(5^{\text{firstexp}-1}+\cdots+5^{\text{kthexpon}-1}\right) \\
& \equiv 2^{\text{counttot}} \cdot 2^{\left(\text{firstexp}-1\right)+\cdots+\left(\text{kthexpon}-1\right)} \quad\left(\text { since } 2^{5^{\text{loopindx}}} \equiv 2 \text { for } \text{loopindx} \geqslant 0\right) \\
& \equiv 2^{\text{firstexp}+\cdots+\text{kthexpon}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Case 2. Some \( \text{firstexp}=0 \), say \( \text{firstexp}=0 \). Now
\[
(1+5 \text{auxiliar})!=(1+5 \text{auxiliar})(5 \text{auxiliar})!,
\]
so \( \text{lastdigit}(1+5 \text{auxiliar}) \equiv(1+5 \text{auxiliar}) \text{lastdigit}(5 \text{auxiliar}) \). But \( \text{lastdigit}(5 \text{auxiliar}) \) is even for \( \text{auxiliar} \geqslant 1 \) since \( (5 \text{auxiliar})! \) is divisible by a higher power of 2 than of 5. But
\[
(1+5 \text{auxiliar}) \cdot(2 \text{loopindy}) \equiv 2 \text{loopindy},
\]
so \( \text{lastdigit}(1+5 \text{auxiliar}) \equiv \text{lastdigit}(5 \text{auxiliar}) \). Letting \( \text{auxiliar}=5^{\text{secondex}-1}+\cdots+5^{\text{kthexpon}-1} \), the proof follows by induction.

(b) The least \( \text{periodic} \geqslant 1 \) for which \( 2^{\text{sumsindex}+\text{periodic}} \equiv 2^{2} \) for all \( \text{sumsindex} \geqslant 1 \) is \( \text{periodic}=4 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem A-6. Let \( pineapple \) be a positive integer, and let \( tortoise(pineapple) \) denote the last nonzero digit in the decimal expansion of \( pineapple! \). For instance, \( tortoise(5)=2 \).
(a) Show that if \( cinnamon, blueberry, \ldots, macadamia \) are distinct nonnegative integers, then \( tortoise\left(5^{cinnamon}+5^{blueberry}+\cdots+5^{macadamia}\right) \) depends only on the sum \( cinnamon+blueberry+\cdots+macadamia \).
(b) Assuming part (a), we can define
\[
armadillo(kangaroo)=tortoise\left(5^{cinnamon}+5^{blueberry}+\cdots+5^{macadamia}\right)
\]
where \( kangaroo=cinnamon+blueberry+\cdots+macadamia \). Find the least positive integer \( marshland \) for which
\[
armadillo(kangaroo)=armadillo(kangaroo+marshland), \quad \text { for all } kangaroo \geqslant 1
\]
or else show that no such \( marshland \) exists.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-6.
(a) All congruences are modulo 10.

Lemma. \( tortoise(5 pineapple) \equiv 2^{pineapple} \, tortoise(pineapple) \).
Proof. We have
(* )
\[
(5 pineapple)!=10^{pineapple} \, pineapple!\prod_{snowflake=0}^{pineapple-1} \frac{(5 \, snowflake+1)(5 \, snowflake+2)(5 \, snowflake+3)(5 \, snowflake+4)}{2} .
\]
If \( snowflake \) is even, then
\[
\frac{1}{2}(5 \, snowflake+1)(5 \, snowflake+2)(5 \, snowflake+3)(5 \, snowflake+4) \equiv \frac{1}{2}(1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 4) \equiv 2,
\]
and if \( snowflake \) is odd, then
\[
\frac{1}{2}(5 \, snowflake+1)(5 \, snowflake+2)(5 \, snowflake+3)(5 \, snowflake+4) \equiv \frac{1}{2}(6 \cdot 7 \cdot 8 \cdot 9) \equiv 2.
\]
Thus the entire product above is congruent to \( 2^{pineapple} \). From (*) it is clear that the largest power of 10 dividing (\( 5 pineapple \) )! is the same as the largest power of 10 dividing \( 10^{pineapple} \, pineapple! \), and the proof follows.

We now show by induction on \( 5^{cinnamon}+\cdots+5^{macadamia} \) that
\[
tortoise\left(5^{cinnamon}+\cdots+5^{macadamia}\right) \equiv 2^{cinnamon+\cdots+macadamia},\]
which depends only on \( cinnamon+\cdots+macadamia \) as desired.
This is true for \( 5^{cinnamon}+\cdots+5^{macadamia}=1 \), since \( tortoise\left(5^{0}\right) \equiv 2^{0} \equiv 1 \).

Case 1. All \( cinnamon, blueberry, \ldots, macadamia>0 \). By the lemma and induction,
\[
\begin{aligned}
tortoise\left(5^{cinnamon}+\cdots+5^{macadamia}\right) & \equiv 2^{5^{cinnamon-1}+\cdots+5^{macadamia-1}} \, tortoise\left(5^{cinnamon-1}+\cdots+5^{macadamia-1}\right) \\ & \equiv 2^{lemonade} \cdot 2^{(cinnamon-1)+\cdots+(macadamia-1)} \quad(\text{since } 2^{5^{\prime}} \equiv 2 \text{ for } snowflake \geqslant 0) \\ & \equiv 2^{cinnamon+\cdots+macadamia} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Case 2. Some \( cinnamon=0 \), say \( cinnamon=0 \). Now
\[
(1+5 \, thunderer)!=(1+5 \, thunderer)(5 \, thunderer)!,
\]
so \( tortoise(1+5 \, thunderer) \equiv (1+5 \, thunderer) \, tortoise(5 \, thunderer) \). But \( tortoise(5 \, thunderer) \) is even for \( thunderer \geqslant 1 \) since \( (5 \, thunderer)! \) is divisible by a higher power of 2 than of 5. But
\[
(1+5 \, thunderer) \cdot (2 \, rainstorm) \equiv 2 \, rainstorm,
\]
so \( tortoise(1+5 \, thunderer) \equiv tortoise(5 \, thunderer) \). Letting \( thunderer = 5^{blueberry-1}+\cdots+5^{macadamia-1} \), the proof follows by induction.

(b) The least \( marshland \geqslant 1 \) for which \( 2^{kangaroo+marshland} \equiv 2^{2} \) for all \( kangaroo \geqslant 1 \) is \( marshland = 4 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem A-6. Let \( fractionalval \) be a positive integer, and let \( wholesumdigit(fractionalval) \) denote the last nonzero digit in the decimal expansion of \( fractionalval! \). For instance, \( wholesumdigit(5)=2 \).
(a) Show that if \( apexindexone, apexindextwo, \ldots, apexindexkay \) are distinct nonnegative integers, then \( wholesumdigit\left(5^{apexindexone}+5^{apexindextwo}+\cdots+5^{apexindexkay}\right) \) depends only on the sum \( apexindexone+apexindextwo+\cdots+apexindexkay \).
(b) Assuming part (a), we can define
\[
inversefunc(difference)=wholesumdigit\left(5^{apexindexone}+5^{apexindextwo}+\cdots+5^{apexindexkay}\right)
\]
where \( difference=apexindexone+apexindextwo+\cdots+apexindexkay \). Find the least positive integer \( aperiodic \) for which
\[
inversefunc(difference)=inversefunc(difference+aperiodic), \quad \text { for all } difference \geqslant 1
\]
or else show that no such \( aperiodic \) exists.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-6.
(a) All congruences are modulo 10 .

Lemma. \( wholesumdigit(5 fractionalval) \equiv 2^{fractionalval} wholesumdigit(fractionalval) \).

Proof. We have
(*)
\[
(5 fractionalval)!=10^{fractionalval} fractionalval!\prod_{maximalindex=0}^{fractionalval-1} \frac{(5 maximalindex+1)(5 maximalindex+2)(5 maximalindex+3)(5 maximalindex+4)}{2} .
\]

If \( maximalindex \) is even, then
\[
\frac{1}{2}(5 maximalindex+1)(5 maximalindex+2)(5 maximalindex+3)(5 maximalindex+4) \equiv \frac{1}{2}(1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 4) \equiv 2,
\]
and if \( maximalindex \) is odd, then
\[
\frac{1}{2}(5 maximalindex+1)(5 maximalindex+2)(5 maximalindex+3)(5 maximalindex+4) \equiv \frac{1}{2}(6 \cdot 7 \cdot 8 \cdot 9) \equiv 2
\]

Thus the entire product above is congruent to \( 2^{fractionalval} \). From (*) it is clear that the largest power of 10 dividing \( (5 fractionalval)! \) is the same as the largest power of 10 dividing \( 10^{fractionalval} fractionalval! \), and the proof follows.

We now show by induction on \( 5^{apexindexone}+\cdots+5^{apexindexkay} \) that
\[
wholesumdigit\left(5^{apexindexone}+\cdots+5^{apexindexkay}\right) \equiv 2^{apexindexone+\cdots+apexindexkay}
\]
(which depends only on \( apexindexone+\cdots+apexindexkay \) as desired).
This is true for \( 5^{apexindexone}+\cdots+5^{apexindexkay}=1 \), since \( wholesumdigit\left(5^{0}\right) \equiv 2^{0} \equiv 1 \).

Case 1. All \( apexindexvar>0 \). By the lemma and induction,
\[
\begin{aligned}
wholesumdigit\left(5^{apexindexone}+\cdots+5^{apexindexkay}\right) & \equiv 2^{5^{apexindexone-1}+\cdots+5^{apexindexkay-1}} wholesumdigit\left(5^{apexindexone-1}+\cdots+5^{apexindexkay-1}\right) \\
& \equiv 2^{singularvalue} \cdot 2^{\left(apexindexone-1\right)+\cdots+\left(apexindexkay-1\right)} \quad\left(\text { since } 2^{maximalindex} \equiv 2 \text { for } maximalindex \geqslant 0\right) \\
& \equiv 2^{apexindexone+\cdots+apexindexkay} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Case 2. Some \( apexindexone=0 \), say \( apexindexone=0 \). Now
\[
(1+5 microamount)!=(1+5 microamount)(5 microamount)!,
\]
so \( wholesumdigit(1+5 microamount) \equiv(1+5 microamount) wholesumdigit(5 microamount) \). But \( wholesumdigit(5 microamount) \) is even for \( microamount \geqslant 1 \) since \( (5 microamount)! \) is divisible by a higher power of 2 than of 5 . But
\[
(1+5 microamount) \cdot(2 residualvar) \equiv 2 residualvar,
\]
so \( wholesumdigit(1+5 microamount) \equiv wholesumdigit(5 microamount) \). Letting \( microamount=5^{apexindextwo-1}+\cdots+5^{apexindexkay-1} \), the proof follows by induction.

(b) The least \( aperiodic \geqslant 1 \) for which \( 2^{difference+aperiodic} \equiv 2^{2} \) for all \( difference \geqslant 1 \) is \( aperiodic=4 \).

garbled_string.question:
Problem A-6. Let \( qzxwvtnp \) be a positive integer, and let \( hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp) \) denote the last nonzero digit in the decimal expansion of \( qzxwvtnp! \). For instance, \( hjgrksla(5)=2 \).
(a) Show that if \( nlskdver, gjtkwpza, \ldots, wqmxzptb \) are distinct nonnegative integers, then \( hjgrksla\left(5^{nlskdver}+5^{gjtkwpza}+\cdots+5^{wqmxzptb}\right) \) depends only on the sum \( nlskdver+gjtkwpza+\cdots+wqmxzptb \).
(b) Assuming part (a), we can define
\[
zfyodmpr(rjsnwevo)=hjgrksla\left(5^{nlskdver}+5^{gjtkwpza}+\cdots+5^{wqmxzptb}\right)
\]
where \( rjsnwevo=nlskdver+gjtkwpza+\cdots+wqmxzptb \). Find the least positive integer \( dktvhwlo \) for which
\[
zfyodmpr(rjsnwevo)=zfyodmpr(rjsnwevo+dktvhwlo), \quad \text { for all } rjsnwevo \geqslant 1
\]
or else show that no such \( dktvhwlo \) exists.
garbled_string.solution:
A-6.
(a) All congruences are modulo 10 .

Lemma. \( hjgrksla(5 qzxwvtnp) \equiv 2^{qzxwvtnp} hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp) \).
Proof. We have
(*)
\[
(5 qzxwvtnp)!=10^{qzxwvtnp} qzxwvtnp!\prod_{xspqgzne=0}^{qzxwvtnp-1} \frac{(5 xspqgzne+1)(5 xspqgzne+2)(5 xspqgzne+3)(5 xspqgzne+4)}{2} .
\]

If \( xspqgzne \) is even, then
\[
\frac{1}{2}(5 xspqgzne+1)(5 xspqgzne+2)(5 xspqgzne+3)(5 xspqgzne+4) \equiv \frac{1}{2}(1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 4) \equiv 2,
\]
and if \( xspqgzne \) is odd, then
\[
\frac{1}{2}(5 xspqgzne+1)(5 xspqgzne+2)(5 xspqgzne+3)(5 xspqgzne+4) \equiv \frac{1}{2}(6 \cdot 7 \cdot 8 \cdot 9) \equiv 2
\]

Thus the entire product above is congruent to \( 2^{qzxwvtnp} \). From (*) it is clear that the largest power of 10 dividing ( \( 5 qzxwvtnp \) )! is the same as the largest power of 10 dividing \( 10^{qzxwvtnp} qzxwvtnp \) !, and the proof follows.

We now show by induction on \( 5^{nlskdver}+\cdots+5^{wqmxzptb} \) that
\[
hjgrksla\left(5^{nlskdver}+\cdots+5^{wqmxzptb}\right) \equiv 2^{nlskdver+\cdots+wqmxzptb}
\]
(which depends only on \( nlskdver+\cdots+wqmxzptb \) as desired).
This is true for \( 5^{nlskdver}+\cdots+5^{wqmxzptb}=1 \), since \( hjgrksla\left(5^{0}\right) \equiv 2^{0} \equiv 1 \).

Case 1. All \( nlskdver, gjtkwpza, \ldots, wqmxzptb>0 \). By the lemma and induction,
\[
\begin{aligned}
hjgrksla\left(5^{nlskdver}+\cdots+5^{wqmxzptb}\right) & \equiv 2^{5^{nlskdver-1}+\cdots+5^{wqmxzptb-1}} hjgrksla\left(5^{nlskdver-1}+\cdots+5^{wqmxzptb-1}\right) \
& \equiv 2^{vblmsgqf} \cdot 2^{\left(nlskdver-1\right)+\cdots+\left(wqmxzptb-1\right)} \quad\left(\text { since } 2^{5^{xspqgzne}} \equiv 2 \text { for } xspqgzne \geqslant 0\right) \\
& \equiv 2^{nlskdver+\cdots+wqmxzptb} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Case 2. Some \( nlskdver=0 \), say \( nlskdver=0 \). Now
\[
(1+5 uytahvrl)!=(1+5 uytahvrl)(5 uytahvrl)!,
\]
so \( hjgrksla(1+5 uytahvrl) \equiv(1+5 uytahvrl) hjgrksla(5 uytahvrl) \). But \( hjgrksla(5 uytahvrl) \) is even for \( uytahvrl \geqslant 1 \) since \( (5 uytahvrl)! \) is divisible by a higher power of 2 than of 5 . But
\[
(1+5 uytahvrl) \cdot(2 cfrdkenu) \equiv 2 cfrdkenu,
\]
so \( hjgrksla(1+5 uytahvrl) \equiv hjgrksla(5 uytahvrl) \). Letting \( uytahvrl=5^{gjtkwpza-1}+\cdots+5^{wqmxzptb-1} \), the proof follows by induction.

(b) The least \( dktvhwlo \geqslant 1 \) for which \( 2^{rjsnwevo+dktvhwlo} \equiv 2^{2} \) for all \( rjsnwevo \geqslant 1 \) is \( dktvhwlo=4 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Fix the modulus \(5^{\,3}=125\).  
For every positive integer \(n\) set  
\[
\lambda(n)=\frac{n!}{5^{\,v_{5}(n!)}}\pmod{125},\qquad
v_{5}(n!)=\left\lfloor\frac{n}{5}\right\rfloor+
          \left\lfloor\frac{n}{5^{2}}\right\rfloor+
          \left\lfloor\frac{n}{5^{3}}\right\rfloor+\cdots .
\]

A non-negative integer is called a \(0/1\)-number if every digit in its
base-\(5\) expansion equals \(0\) or \(1\).
For a finite set \(A\subset\mathbf Z_{\ge 0}\) put  
\[
\begin{aligned}
N(A)&=\sum_{a\in A}5^{a}\quad(\text{so }N(A)\text{ is a }0/1\text{-number}),\\[2pt]
w(A)&=\lvert A\rvert ,\qquad
s(A)=\sum_{a\in A}a,\\[2pt]
T_{A}(a)&=\sum_{\substack{b\in A\\ b>a}}5^{\,b-a-1}\qquad (a\in A),\\[2pt]
\theta(A)&=\dfrac{N(A)-w(A)}{4}.
\end{aligned}
\]
(The quotient \(\theta(A)\) is an integer since each power \(5^{a}\)
satisfies \(5^{a}\equiv1\pmod4\).)

(a)  Prove the following \(125\)-adic factorisation
\[
\boxed{\;
      \lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\equiv
      24^{\,\theta(A)}
      \prod_{a\in A}\bigl(1+5\,T_{A}(a)\bigr)
      \pmod{125}
     \;}.
\]

(b)  Reduce the congruence in part (a) modulo \(5\) and show that the
residue of \(\lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\) modulo \(5\) depends only on
\(s(A)\).  Determine the least positive period \(p\) of the function
\[
s\;\longmapsto\;\lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\pmod 5
\qquad\bigl(s=s(A)\bigr).
\]

(c)  Give explicit sets \(A,B\) with  
\(w(A)=w(B)\) and \(s(A)=s(B)\) but  
\[
\lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\not\equiv\lambda\!\bigl(N(B)\bigr)\pmod{125},
\]
thus showing that the pair \((w,s)\) no longer determines
\(\lambda(n)\) once one works modulo \(125\).

-------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout, congruences are understood modulo \(125\); the
symbol \(v_{5}(\,\cdot\,)\) denotes the usual \(5\)-adic valuation.

1.  A basic \(5\)-adic recursion for \(\lambda(n)\)

Write \(n=5q+d\) with \(0\le d\le4\).  Separating the last block of five
factors,
\[
n!=(5q)!\prod_{j=1}^{d}(5q+j).
\]
Inside \((5q)!\) split every complete \(5\)-block:
\[
(5q)!=5^{\,q}\,q!\prod_{i=1}^{q}(5i-1)(5i-2)(5i-3)(5i-4).
\]
Because \(v_{5}\bigl((5q)!\bigr)=q+v_{5}(q!)\), division by
\(5^{\,v_{5}(n!)}\) yields
\[
\boxed{\;
   \lambda(5q+d)=\lambda(q)\,C(q)\,D(q,d)
 \;}
\]
with
\[
C(q)=\prod_{i=1}^{q}(5i-1)(5i-2)(5i-3)(5i-4),\qquad
D(q,d)=\prod_{j=1}^{d}(5q+j).
\]

2.  Evaluation of \(C(q)\) modulo \(125\)

Lemma 1. For every \(q\ge0\) one has  
\[
\boxed{\;C(q)\equiv24^{\,q}\pmod{125}\;}.
\]

Proof.  Put \(P(x)=(x-1)(x-2)(x-3)(x-4)=x^{4}-10x^{3}+35x^{2}-50x+24\).
Substituting \(x=5i\) shows \(P(5i)\equiv24\pmod{125}\); consequently
every factor in \(C(q)\) is congruent to \(24\), and the lemma follows.
\(\square\)

3.  Iterating the recursion along the base-\(5\) digits of
\(N(A)\)

Let  
\[
N_{0}=N(A),\qquad
N_{j+1}=\left\lfloor\frac{N_{j}}{5}\right\rfloor ,\qquad
d_{j}=N_{j}-5N_{j+1}\in\{0,1\}.
\]
Because the base-\(5\) expansion of \(N(A)\) uses only the digits
\(0,1\), we have \(d_{j}=\mathbf1_{(j\in A)}\) and \(N_{j}=0\) for
\(j\gg0\).

Using the recursion and Lemma 1,
\[
\lambda(N_{j})\equiv
\lambda(N_{j+1})\,24^{\,N_{j+1}}\,(5N_{j+1}+1)^{\,d_{j}}
\pmod{125}.
\]
Iterating until \(N_{m}=0\) gives
\[
\lambda(N(A))\equiv
24^{\,S}\,
\prod_{j\ge0}(5N_{j+1}+1)^{\,d_{j}}
\pmod{125},
\qquad
S=\sum_{j\ge0}N_{j+1}.
\]

4.  Evaluation of the exponent \(S\)

Because \(N_{j}=\sum_{k\ge j}d_{k}5^{\,k-j}\),
\[
S=\sum_{j\ge0}\;\sum_{k\ge j+1}d_{k}5^{\,k-j-1}
   =\sum_{k\ge1}d_{k}\frac{5^{\,k}-1}{4}
   =\frac{1}{4}\Bigl(N(A)-d_{0}-\bigl(w(A)-d_{0}\bigr)\Bigr)
   =\frac{N(A)-w(A)}{4}.
\]
Hence \(S=\theta(A)\).

5.  Identification of the factors \((5N_{j+1}+1)\)

For an index \(a\) with \(d_{a}=1\) we have
\[
N_{a+1}=\sum_{\substack{b\in A\\ b>a}}5^{\,b-a-1}=T_{A}(a),
\]
so \(5N_{a+1}+1=1+5T_{A}(a)\).  Splitting the product over those indices
yields
\[
\boxed{\;
      \lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\equiv
      24^{\,\theta(A)}
      \prod_{a\in A}\bigl(1+5\,T_{A}(a)\bigr)
      \pmod{125}
     \;},
\]
establishing part (a).

6.  Consequences modulo \(5\)

Reducing the boxed formula modulo \(5\) gives  
\[
\lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\equiv(-1)^{\,\theta(A)}\pmod5
\]
because \(24\equiv-1\pmod5\) and \(1+5T_{A}(a)\equiv1\pmod5\).

Parity connection.  For \(a\ge1\)
\[
\frac{5^{\,a}-1}{4}\equiv
\begin{cases}
0\pmod2,& a\text{ even},\\
1\pmod2,& a\text{ odd},
\end{cases}
\]
so
\[
\theta(A)\equiv\sum_{a\in A}a\equiv s(A)\pmod2.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{\;
   \lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\equiv(-1)^{\,s(A)}\pmod5
 \;}.
\]

Since \((-1)^{\,s+2}=(-1)^{\,s}\), the least positive period of the map
\(s\mapsto\lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\pmod5\) equals  
\[
\boxed{\,p=2\,}.
\]

7.  Non-uniqueness modulo \(125\)

Choose  
\[
A=\{0,3\},\qquad B=\{1,2\}.
\]
Both sets satisfy  
\[
w(A)=w(B)=2,\qquad s(A)=s(B)=3,
\]
yet they behave differently modulo \(125\).

Set \(N_{A}=N(A)=1+125=126\) and \(N_{B}=N(B)=5+25=30\).

First set \(A\).
\[
\theta(A)=\frac{126-2}{4}=31,\quad
\prod_{a\in A}\bigl(1+5T_{A}(a)\bigr)\equiv1,
\]
whence
\[
\lambda(126)\equiv24^{31}\equiv24\pmod{125}.
\]

Second set \(B\).
\[
\theta(B)=\frac{30-2}{4}=7,\quad
\prod_{a\in B}\bigl(1+5T_{B}(a)\bigr)\equiv(1+5\cdot1)=6,
\]
so
\[
\lambda(30)\equiv24^{7}\cdot6\equiv49\cdot6\equiv44\pmod{125}.
\]

Thus  
\[
\boxed{\;
  \lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)=24\not\equiv44=\lambda\!\bigl(N(B)\bigr)\pmod{125}
 \;},
\]
although \(w(A)=w(B)\) and \(s(A)=s(B)\).  Part (c) is proved.

-------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher modulus.  
The original problem works modulo 10; the variant demands work modulo 10^r for arbitrary r.  This forces simultaneous control of congruences mod 2^r and mod 5^r and introduces primitive-root arguments, Euler totients, and Chinese-Remainder synthesis that were absent before.

2.  Explicit 10-adic factorisation.  
To establish the key lemma ℓ_r(5n)=12^{n}ℓ_r(n) one must handle the entire block  
(5j+1)(5j+2)(5j+3)(5j+4)/2  modulo 10^r, rather than merely modulo 10; keeping track of the 2-adic valuation inside this product is considerably subtler.

3.  Two-parameter dependence.  
Unlike the original problem (where the answer depends solely on s), here the answer depends on both s and the cardinality k.  Proving minimality (part (c)) requires a delicate contradiction using the coprimality of C_k with 5 and shows that no purely one-dimensional period exists.

4.  Period computation for all r.  
Pinpointing p_r relies on the fact that 2 is a primitive root modulo 5^r, an advanced number-theoretic fact; verifying minimality requires combining this with 2-adic considerations.

Overall, the enhanced variant compels the solver to blend 10-adic lifting, primitive-root theory, valuations, and the Chinese Remainder Theorem—elements far beyond the scope of the original problem, and impossible to dispatch by simple pattern-matching.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix the modulus \(5^{\,3}=125\).  
For every positive integer \(n\) set  
\[
\lambda(n)=\frac{n!}{5^{\,v_{5}(n!)}}\pmod{125},\qquad
v_{5}(n!)=\left\lfloor\frac{n}{5}\right\rfloor+
          \left\lfloor\frac{n}{5^{2}}\right\rfloor+
          \left\lfloor\frac{n}{5^{3}}\right\rfloor+\cdots .
\]

A non-negative integer is called a \(0/1\)-number if every digit in its
base-\(5\) expansion equals \(0\) or \(1\).
For a finite set \(A\subset\mathbf Z_{\ge 0}\) put  
\[
\begin{aligned}
N(A)&=\sum_{a\in A}5^{a}\quad(\text{so }N(A)\text{ is a }0/1\text{-number}),\\[2pt]
w(A)&=\lvert A\rvert ,\qquad
s(A)=\sum_{a\in A}a,\\[2pt]
T_{A}(a)&=\sum_{\substack{b\in A\\ b>a}}5^{\,b-a-1}\qquad (a\in A),\\[2pt]
\theta(A)&=\dfrac{N(A)-w(A)}{4}.
\end{aligned}
\]
(The quotient \(\theta(A)\) is an integer since each power \(5^{a}\)
satisfies \(5^{a}\equiv1\pmod4\).)

(a)  Prove the following \(125\)-adic factorisation
\[
\boxed{\;
      \lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\equiv
      24^{\,\theta(A)}
      \prod_{a\in A}\bigl(1+5\,T_{A}(a)\bigr)
      \pmod{125}
     \;}.
\]

(b)  Reduce the congruence in part (a) modulo \(5\) and show that the
residue of \(\lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\) modulo \(5\) depends only on
\(s(A)\).  Determine the least positive period \(p\) of the function
\[
s\;\longmapsto\;\lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\pmod 5
\qquad\bigl(s=s(A)\bigr).
\]

(c)  Give explicit sets \(A,B\) with  
\(w(A)=w(B)\) and \(s(A)=s(B)\) but  
\[
\lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\not\equiv\lambda\!\bigl(N(B)\bigr)\pmod{125},
\]
thus showing that the pair \((w,s)\) no longer determines
\(\lambda(n)\) once one works modulo \(125\).

-------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout, congruences are understood modulo \(125\); the
symbol \(v_{5}(\,\cdot\,)\) denotes the usual \(5\)-adic valuation.

1.  A basic \(5\)-adic recursion for \(\lambda(n)\)

Write \(n=5q+d\) with \(0\le d\le4\).  Separating the last block of five
factors,
\[
n!=(5q)!\prod_{j=1}^{d}(5q+j).
\]
Inside \((5q)!\) split every complete \(5\)-block:
\[
(5q)!=5^{\,q}\,q!\prod_{i=1}^{q}(5i-1)(5i-2)(5i-3)(5i-4).
\]
Because \(v_{5}\bigl((5q)!\bigr)=q+v_{5}(q!)\), division by
\(5^{\,v_{5}(n!)}\) yields
\[
\boxed{\;
   \lambda(5q+d)=\lambda(q)\,C(q)\,D(q,d)
 \;}
\]
with
\[
C(q)=\prod_{i=1}^{q}(5i-1)(5i-2)(5i-3)(5i-4),\qquad
D(q,d)=\prod_{j=1}^{d}(5q+j).
\]

2.  Evaluation of \(C(q)\) modulo \(125\)

Lemma 1. For every \(q\ge0\) one has  
\[
\boxed{\;C(q)\equiv24^{\,q}\pmod{125}\;}.
\]

Proof.  Put \(P(x)=(x-1)(x-2)(x-3)(x-4)=x^{4}-10x^{3}+35x^{2}-50x+24\).
Substituting \(x=5i\) shows \(P(5i)\equiv24\pmod{125}\); consequently
every factor in \(C(q)\) is congruent to \(24\), and the lemma follows.
\(\square\)

3.  Iterating the recursion along the base-\(5\) digits of
\(N(A)\)

Let  
\[
N_{0}=N(A),\qquad
N_{j+1}=\left\lfloor\frac{N_{j}}{5}\right\rfloor ,\qquad
d_{j}=N_{j}-5N_{j+1}\in\{0,1\}.
\]
Because the base-\(5\) expansion of \(N(A)\) uses only the digits
\(0,1\), we have \(d_{j}=\mathbf1_{(j\in A)}\) and \(N_{j}=0\) for
\(j\gg0\).

Using the recursion and Lemma 1,
\[
\lambda(N_{j})\equiv
\lambda(N_{j+1})\,24^{\,N_{j+1}}\,(5N_{j+1}+1)^{\,d_{j}}
\pmod{125}.
\]
Iterating until \(N_{m}=0\) gives
\[
\lambda(N(A))\equiv
24^{\,S}\,
\prod_{j\ge0}(5N_{j+1}+1)^{\,d_{j}}
\pmod{125},
\qquad
S=\sum_{j\ge0}N_{j+1}.
\]

4.  Evaluation of the exponent \(S\)

Because \(N_{j}=\sum_{k\ge j}d_{k}5^{\,k-j}\),
\[
S=\sum_{j\ge0}\;\sum_{k\ge j+1}d_{k}5^{\,k-j-1}
   =\sum_{k\ge1}d_{k}\frac{5^{\,k}-1}{4}
   =\frac{1}{4}\Bigl(N(A)-d_{0}-\bigl(w(A)-d_{0}\bigr)\Bigr)
   =\frac{N(A)-w(A)}{4}.
\]
Hence \(S=\theta(A)\).

5.  Identification of the factors \((5N_{j+1}+1)\)

For an index \(a\) with \(d_{a}=1\) we have
\[
N_{a+1}=\sum_{\substack{b\in A\\ b>a}}5^{\,b-a-1}=T_{A}(a),
\]
so \(5N_{a+1}+1=1+5T_{A}(a)\).  Splitting the product over those indices
yields
\[
\boxed{\;
      \lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\equiv
      24^{\,\theta(A)}
      \prod_{a\in A}\bigl(1+5\,T_{A}(a)\bigr)
      \pmod{125}
     \;},
\]
establishing part (a).

6.  Consequences modulo \(5\)

Reducing the boxed formula modulo \(5\) gives  
\[
\lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\equiv(-1)^{\,\theta(A)}\pmod5
\]
because \(24\equiv-1\pmod5\) and \(1+5T_{A}(a)\equiv1\pmod5\).

Parity connection.  For \(a\ge1\)
\[
\frac{5^{\,a}-1}{4}\equiv
\begin{cases}
0\pmod2,& a\text{ even},\\
1\pmod2,& a\text{ odd},
\end{cases}
\]
so
\[
\theta(A)\equiv\sum_{a\in A}a\equiv s(A)\pmod2.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{\;
   \lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\equiv(-1)^{\,s(A)}\pmod5
 \;}.
\]

Since \((-1)^{\,s+2}=(-1)^{\,s}\), the least positive period of the map
\(s\mapsto\lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)\pmod5\) equals  
\[
\boxed{\,p=2\,}.
\]

7.  Non-uniqueness modulo \(125\)

Choose  
\[
A=\{0,3\},\qquad B=\{1,2\}.
\]
Both sets satisfy  
\[
w(A)=w(B)=2,\qquad s(A)=s(B)=3,
\]
yet they behave differently modulo \(125\).

Set \(N_{A}=N(A)=1+125=126\) and \(N_{B}=N(B)=5+25=30\).

First set \(A\).
\[
\theta(A)=\frac{126-2}{4}=31,\quad
\prod_{a\in A}\bigl(1+5T_{A}(a)\bigr)\equiv1,
\]
whence
\[
\lambda(126)\equiv24^{31}\equiv24\pmod{125}.
\]

Second set \(B\).
\[
\theta(B)=\frac{30-2}{4}=7,\quad
\prod_{a\in B}\bigl(1+5T_{B}(a)\bigr)\equiv(1+5\cdot1)=6,
\]
so
\[
\lambda(30)\equiv24^{7}\cdot6\equiv49\cdot6\equiv44\pmod{125}.
\]

Thus  
\[
\boxed{\;
  \lambda\!\bigl(N(A)\bigr)=24\not\equiv44=\lambda\!\bigl(N(B)\bigr)\pmod{125}
 \;},
\]
although \(w(A)=w(B)\) and \(s(A)=s(B)\).  Part (c) is proved.

-------------------------------------------------------------
